coredata.dc:title: In situ growth of Z-scheme CuS/CuSCN heterojunction to passivate surface defects and enhance charge transport
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ning, Pei
Liang, Junhui
Li, Linghui
Chen, Da
Qin, Laishun
Yao, Xin
Chen, Huayu
Huang, Yuexiang
coredata.dc:description: Copper thiocyanate (CuSCN) has been considered as a promising hole transport material (HTMs), attributing to its inherent stability, low-cost, and suitable energy levels. To make it more attractive in practical applications, the drawbacks of CuSCN in poor charge transport and serious defect recombination are bottlenecks that need to be overcome. In this work, we propose an effective strategy of in-situ decorating CuSCN with copper sulfide quantum dots (CuS QDs), a simple one-step electrochemical deposition process, to solve these issues. Compared with the pristine CuSCN, the constructed Z-Scheme heterojunction of CuS QDs/CuSCN can significantly promote charge transport and restrict recombination. In addition, the decorated CuS QDs can not only passivate defects of CuSCN, but also provide more contacting sites to facilitate hole injection when employing as HTM. As a result, the average bulk charge lifetime was improved from 0.37 ms to 0.47 ms, and the surface recombination rate constant was suppressed. We believe that the excellent performances will pave it toward practical device applications, including solar cells, photocatalysis, photoelectrochemical sensors, and light-emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuSCN
Hole transport layer
Heterojunction
Suppressed charge recombination
Accelerated charge transport

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51872271, 51972294, 51972291) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20F040007, LQ19F040004, LY19E020003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: . (a) The XRD patterns of pure CuS QDs. (b) The low magnification (left) and high magnification (right) TEM images of CuS QDs. (c) The XRD patterns of CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN composite. (d) Low magnification (left) and high magnification (right) TEM images of CuS QDs/CuSCN.
. The XPS spectra of pure CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN composition. (a) The XPS survey spectra. (b) Cu 2p. (c) S 2p.
. The schematic illustration for the charge transport and transfer in CuS QDs/CuSCN composite.

The optical and photoelectrochemical properties of CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN samples. (a) Photocurrent-voltage curves. (b) Chopped chronoamperometry curves measured at −0.2 V vs. RHE. (c) UV–VIS absorption spectra (upper) and IPCE (lower) collected at −0.2 V vs. RHE.

. The photoelectrochemical performances of CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN composition. (a) Extracted charge density (obtained by integrating the short circuit current after a certain decay time) as a function of decay time. (b) The IMPS measured at constant potential of −0.2 V vs. RHE.
. The photoelectrochemical performances of CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN composition. (a) Mott-Schottky curves. (b) EIS spectra.

head.ce:title: In situ growth of Z-scheme CuS/CuSCN heterojunction to passivate surface defects and enhance charge transport

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemistry, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 300018, Zhejiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemistry, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 300018, Zhejiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 300018, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Optical and Electronic Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 300018, Zhejiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In-situ grown CuS QDs/CuSCN Z-Scheme heterojunction with a simple one-step electrodeposition technique to overcome the bottlenecks of poor charge transport and abundant defect recombination of CuSCN.

Copper thiocyanate (CuSCN) has been considered as a promising hole transport material (HTMs), attributing to its inherent stability, low-cost, and suitable energy levels. To make it more attractive in practical applications, the drawbacks of CuSCN in poor charge transport and serious defect recombination are bottlenecks that need to be overcome. In this work, we propose an effective strategy of in-situ decorating CuSCN with copper sulfide quantum dots (CuS QDs), a simple one-step electrochemical deposition process, to solve these issues. Compared with the pristine CuSCN, the constructed Z-Scheme heterojunction of CuS QDs/CuSCN can significantly promote charge transport and restrict recombination. In addition, the decorated CuS QDs can not only passivate defects of CuSCN, but also provide more contacting sites to facilitate hole injection when employing as HTM. As a result, the average bulk charge lifetime was improved from 0.37 ms to 0.47 ms, and the surface recombination rate constant was suppressed. We believe that the excellent performances will pave it toward practical device applications, including solar cells, photocatalysis, photoelectrochemical sensors, and light-emitting diodes.

ce:section.ce:para: Balanced charge transfer or injection rate between electron transport materials (ETMs) and hole transport materials (HTMs) is critical in determining the device performances, especially for optoelectronic detectors, solar cells, light-emitting diodes, multi light-absorber semiconductor devices, etc. [1–4] Unfortunately, the HTMs usually exhibit a slower hole transfer rate than that of ETMs, blocking its further progress. To address these concerns, various organic HTMs are retained, including spiroOMeTAD, polymer-based poly (triarylamine) (PTAA), polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) and so on [5–7], and already achieve huge success. Nevertheless, the prohibitively high-cost with drawbacks in low hole-mobility and instability issues impede severely their potential large-scale application. Therefore, exploring an alternative HTM is urgent and essential before its commercial deployment.

As promising alternative HTMs, inorganic materials, mainly dominated by the transition metal-based compounds, have received overwhelming attention [8]. Among them, CuSCN has been regarded as the most potential candidate, due to its higher hole mobility (10-2 ~ 10-1 cm2·V−1·s−1) than typical organic HTMs (spiroOMeTAD of ~ 10-5 cm2·V−1·s−1 and PTAA of ~ 10-3 cm2·V−1·s−1), suitable band energy level to collect hole and block electron, good thermal stability, extremely low fabrication cost and temperature [9]. In addition, the low parasitic absorption of CuSCN is also a concern for optoelectronic devices. With the unprecedented development of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) over the past years, stabilized photovoltaic conversion efficiencies (PCE) of over 20% have been yielded by employing CuSCN as HTM [2]. It is noteworthy that, the same results are also discovered in the fields of photodetector and LED [1,10–12], indicating the enormous application potential.

Despite its enticing benefits, the CuSCN based devices generally deliver comparatively poorer performances than that involved organic HTMs. The main challenges that remain to be addressed include: 1) low charge collection efficiency arising from poor conductivity; 2) serious charge recombination originated from intrinsic defect sites or formed interfacial defects with adjacent layer [6]. Considerable efforts have been put forward to tackle all these issues, which can be categorized into two types: 1) Doping engineering to improve hole transport and extraction capability. For instance, Hou [9] and Jin [13] doped 2,3,5,6-Tetrafluoro-7,7,8,8-tetracyanoquinodime-thane (F4-TCNQ) into CuSCN to improve the electronic properties of HTMs, and hence to reduce the energy level offset and hole transport resistance at the interface of CuSCN and adjacent layers. 2) Interface engineering to mitigate the defect formation at the interface of CuSCN and adjacent layer. For example, Park and co-workers [14] employed polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as a crosslinking interlayer to prevent the formation of interfacial defects, which in turn boosted hole transport property. Meanwhile, Lee [15] and wang [16] improved the interfacial contact between CuSCN and adjacent layer by introducing functional molecules of 3-pyridylisothiocyanate (Pr-ITC), phenylene-1, 4-diisothiocyanate (Ph-DITC) and two-dimensional antimonene quantum sheets respectively, to passivate the defects caused by damage and poor contact with adjacent layer. As a result, the fabricated CuSCN-based PSCs exhibited almost the same PCE as that of spiroOMeTAD. Though the charge transport and interfacial defects of CuSCN are vastly mitigated by doping and interface engineering, the intrinsic defects and additionally defects derived from the introduced crosslinking interlayer are still non-negligible.

In-situ grown heterojunction has been confirmed as an effective strategy to passivate intrinsic defects and enhance charge transport [17,18], with less induced novel defects. The similar class of inorganic copper-based semiconductors, such as copper ionic compounds (CuS, copper iodide (CuI), Cu2ZnSnS4 (CZTS), CuInS2), copper oxide (CuOx) and delafossite CuGaO2, are suitable candidate to construct heterojunction with CuSCN, due to their high hole mobility, noticeably economical and chemically stable [8,19–21]. In addition to the characteristic mentioned above, lattice defects, usually regarded as recombination centers and caused by the mismatch of lattice constants between different semiconductors, are also not to be neglected [22]. From the detailed lattice parameter shown in Table S1, CuS exhibits the most approximate lattice constants with CuSCN among various copper-based materials above. Taking all these factors into consideration, we propose a new strategy of in situ grown CuS quantum dots (QDs)/CuSCN heterojunction to simultaneously accelerate charge transport, passivate intrinsic defects and suppress the formation of interfacial defects. The results exhibit that the charge collection efficiency is dramatically improved by constructing CuS QDs/CuSCN heterojunction. Based on the chemical composition and photoelectrochemical analysis, the charge transport mechanism of CuS QDs/CuSCN heterojunction is revealed.

Firstly, CuS quantum dots were synthesized according to the previous report [23]. After that, the as-prepared CuS QDs were ultrasonically dispersed in 200 mL deionized water for 2 h to form CuS QDs dispersion (16 mg/L). Afterward, 2.4 mmol copper pentahydrate, 0.6 mmol potassium thiocyanate (KSCN) and 2.4 mmol ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were added into the CuS QDs dispersion. Before electrodepositing, the FTO substrates were ultrasonically washed with deionized water, isopropanol, deionized water and absolute ethanol for 15 min respectively, and then irradiated with an ultraviolet ozone machine for 15 min. Subsequently, CuS QDs/CuSCN was fabricated via electrochemical deposition in a standard three-electrode, using FTO as the working electrode, platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode and the above-prepared CuS QDs precursor as electrolyte. During the preparation process, the electrodeposition potential and charge were controlled at −0.4 V vs. SCE and 80 mC by Shanghai Chenhua Electrochemical Instrumentation (CHI660E). For reference, a CuSCN film was synthesized using a precursor solution without CuS quantum dots.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was measured by a Bruker D2 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (
λ
= 0.154 nm) in the 2θ range of 10°–70°. The morphological features and microstructures were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU 8010) and field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100), respectively. In addition, the CuSCN or CuS QDs/CuSCN was carefully peeled off with a clean blade before being photographed, and then sonicated for 2 h in alcohol. Afterwards, a small amount of the drop was sucked into the copper mesh. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by PHI5000 Versa Probe.

The photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode configuration immersed in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution under illuminance of 300 W/m2 using UV LED lamp (LED UVR365, ZAHNER) as light source. The potential of working electrode was controlled with Zener potentiostat/galvanostat (CIMPS-2, ZAHNER-elektrik GmbH & Co. KG, Germany). The entire test was carried out in a black box. For photocurrent measurement, the light source was switched by setting the LED driver (PP211, ZAHNER-elektrik GmbH & Co. KG, Germany). The Mott-Schottky plots were collected at a frequency of 5 kHz and amplitude of 5 mV. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed under illumination conditions with a set frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz and amplitude of 5 mV. The charge extraction method is used to test the number of excited carriers in the sample. The intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) measured the photocurrent of the film in the range of 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 80 mV under 300 W/m2 of UV light.

To verify the successful synthesis of CuS QDs, XRD and TEM were carried out to characterize the crystal structure and microstructure. The diffraction peaks of pure CuS QDs (Fig. 1
a) centered at 2θ angles of 29.27, 31.78, 32.85, 47.78 and 47.94° were indexed to the characteristic peaks of CuS (JCPDS No. 06–0464), corresponding to the diffraction planes (102), (103), (006), (107) and (110), respectively [23]. In addition, the sizes in a range of 2–4 nm ( Fig. S1 ) were obtained with lattice distance of 0.272 nm (Fig. 1 b), which was matched well with the (006) facet in CuS lattice, further indicating that the CuS QDs were successfully prepared.

After the mixing procedure, the characteristic peak located at 2θ angle of 16.16° (Fig. 1 c) was typically assigned to the (003) crystal plane of β-CuSCN (JCPDS No. 29–0581), which was consistent with our previous reports [17,18]. Meanwhile, the other prominent peaks were corresponded to FTO substrate. It’s worth noting that no obvious diffraction peaks correlated with CuS QDs were observed. This was probably owing to its relatively low concentration and high dispersity in the composites. Similar phenomena also happened in other binary or ternary systems [24,25]. In order to prove this, Fig. 1 d presented the low and high magnification TEM images of CuS QDs/CuSCN composite. A large number of CuS QDs were evenly anchored on both the outer and inner β-CuSCN nanorods. Moreover, the characteristic lattice fringes ascribed to the (103) plane of CuS QDs and (101) plane of CuSCN were also observed, which was consistent with the results of XRD. All of these clarified that CuS QDs/CuSCN composites have been developed successfully. Besides, it was commendable that no aggregation of CuS QDs could be observed. Last but not least, without changing the nanorod morphology of CuSCN, the CuS QDs/CuSCN composite exhibited a roughened surface with larger surface area (as illustrated in Fig. S2 ), which was beneficial to enhance contact area and accelerate charge transport.

XPS was further performed to investigate the chemical composition and elemental valence state. Notably, the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2
a) collected from pure CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN composition demonstrated the existence of Sn and O elements except the expected Cu, S, C and N elements, which was likely associated primarily with the FTO substrate rather than any other byproduct or impurity. Meanwhile, the peak separation between the Cu 2p3/2 and the primary Cu LMM peak matched well with the value of Cu+ for CuSCN [26]. Besides, the major peaks in C1s at 285.7 eV and N 1 s at 398.3 eV for CuSCN, C1s at 285.2 eV and N 1 s at 398.0 eV ( Fig. S3 ) for CuS QDs/CuSCN were assigned to the sp-state carbon in its -C
≡
N form [27,28], confirming the existence of CuSCN.

To further confirm the presence of Cu (I) (Cu+) for CuSCN and Cu (II) (Cu2+) for CuS QDs, high-resolution spectra of Cu 2p were illustrated in Fig. 2 b. The Cu 2p spectra were almost identical, with Cu 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks centered at binding energies of 932.4 and 952.3 eV for CuSCN, 931.9 and 951.9 eV for CuS QDs/CuSCN, which were consistent with the typical values of Cu+ in CuSCN [29]. An additional peak with binding energy of 933.1 eV in CuS QDs/CuSCN was attributed to the satellite peak of Cu2+ state in CuS QDs [30]. In addition, two main peaks located at 163.1 and 164.3 eV for pure CuSCN, 163.0 and 164.2 eV for CuS QDs/CuSCN appeared in the high-resolution spectra of S 2p (Fig. 2 c), which could be ascribed to a sulfur atom in SC form of CuSCN [27]. As expected, a weak peak at 161.5 eV was observed in CuS QDs/CuSCN, corresponding to metal sulphides (M−S) peaks or S2- species [30]. All of the above results revealed that the CuS QDs and CuSCN nanorods were successfully hybridized.

Apart from the chemical composition and elemental valence state deduced by binding energy positions, more careful inspection should be focused on the binding energy shifts of CuS QDs/CuSCN, which indicated a strong electronic interaction between CuSCN and CuS QDs. Apparently, in the case of CuS QDs/CuSCN, the binding energy of Cu 2p, S 2p, N 1 s and C 1 s shifted to lower positions as compared to the pure CuSCN. Generally, the reduced binding energy implied the enhancement of electron density.[24,31–34] In other words, the electrons were transferred from CuS QDs to CuSCN, resulting in the increased electron density of CuSCN, and thus bringing about a decrease in binding energy. This may be related to the interfacial interaction of constructed CuS QDs/CuSCN heterojunction, which was favorable to accelerate charge transport and inhibit charge recombination.

To clarify the charge transport mechanism of CuS QDs/CuSCN heterojunction, a schematic diagram was presented in Fig. 3 . The band energy position of CuSCN was obtained by previous reports [18]. Additionally, the potentials of conduction band edge (ECB) and valence band edge (EVB) of CuS QDs could be calculated via the following equations [35]:
(1)
E
CB
=
E
VB
-
E g
(2)
E
VB
= X -
E e
+ 0.5 ×
E g
where X and Ee were the electronegativity (about 5.28 eV [36]) and the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV), Eg was the abbreviation of band gap. Among them, Eg could be deduced by the emission peak from PL spectrum ( Fig. S4 ), [37–39] due to the fact that the emission peak was attributed to the emission of bandgap transition with the energy of light approximately equal to the bandgap energy of CuS QDs (477 nm, 2.60 eV). From the conduction band and valance band positions point of view, CuS QDs/CuSCN was usually attached to the type-II heterojunction. As expected, the photogenerated electrons were transferred from CuSCN to CuS QDs. As a matter of fact, the electron transfer direction was completely opposite as mentioned above. From the perspective of charge transfer, this could be assigned to the direct Z-Scheme heterojunction [40–43]. What’s more, an internal built-in electric field directing from CuS QDs to CuSCN was formed, which is conducive to facilitate charge transport and restrict bulk charge recombination.

To further verify the concept that the CuS QDs/CuSCN heterojunction could improve charge transport and restrict charge recombination, we employed the optical and photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements (Fig. 4 ) to quantify the charge transport efficiency. As we all know, the photocurrent density of photoelectrode could be expressed by the equation [44,45]:
(3)
J
ph
=
J abs
×
η
transport
×
η
transfer
where Jph was the effective photocurrent density and collected by the PEC measurements; Jabs represented the photon-absorption rate expressed as current density and depended on the number of absorbed photons;
η
transport and
η
transfer were the charge transport and transfer efficiency, respectively.

As displayed in Fig. 4 a, a considerable improvement in the saturation current density was obtained for CuS QDs/CuSCN, in comparison to the pure CuSCN. Meanwhile, obvious transient spikes could be observed for both samples with low external applied bias. This phenomenon may be caused by the poor
η
transfer across the semiconductor/electrolyte interface [46,47]. However, when the externally applied bias was enough to overcome the slow kinetics or overpotential, no photocurrent transient spikes appeared in the saturation region, as shown in Fig. 4 b. In this regard, the charge transfer efficiency could be assumed to be ~ 100% (
η
transfer = 1). Thereby, the saturation photocurrent density (JSC ) could be determined using the following equation:
(4)
J
SC
=
J abs
×
η
transport

Among them, the Jabs of both CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN should be similar, due to the nearly same light harvesting (Fig. 4 c upper). This allowed one to directly calculate the charge transport efficiency from the measured saturation photocurrent density. As expected, CuS QDs/CuSCN presented almost twice higher Jsc than pure CuSCN, suggesting that the introduced CuS QDs could improve charge transport efficiency. Besides, the improved photocurrent density may be attributed to the passivation effect of CuS QDs, which could restrain defect recombination [48]. Furthermore, The stability of CuS QDs/CuSCN and CuSCN under realistic operational conditions, which is the key for the industrialization application, was also studied during the 4 h chronoamperometry test in Na2SO4 electrolyte with pH = 6, as shown in Fig. S5 . From the photograph, we can observe that both CuS QDs/CuSCN and CuSCN exhibited shows long-term stability over the course of the 4 h reaction.

The charge transport efficiency could be also deduced by incident-photon-to-current-conversion efficiency (IPCE), which was a powerful tool to understand the interplay between light harvesting and effective charge, as follows [44]:
(5)
IPCE =
η
abs
×
η
transport
×
η
transfer
where
η
abs mean the number of electron-hole pairs generated per incident photon. Similar to the aforementioned properties, the IPCE of both CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN were independent from
η
abs and
η
transfer . Therefore, the uniformly enhanced IPCE values over the whole wavelength range for CuS QDs/CuSCN indicated that the separation and transportation of photoexcited charges were very efficient [49].

Based on the discussion above, it’s reasonable to hypothesize that the improved charge transport efficiency was owed to the cooperative effects of constructing heterojunction and reduced defect density caused by CuS QDs passivation. In order to certify our assumption, extracted charge as a function of decay time and IMPS were conducted to investigate the charge lifetime, which were normally considered as direct evidence for charge recombination. As exhibited in Fig. 5
a, the extracted charge density (~28 μC/cm2) in CuS QDs/CuSCN at decay time of 0 s was nearly 3.7 times higher than that of pure CuSCN, confirming that the introduced CuS QDs could significantly suppress charge recombination, and thus resulting in abundantly accumulated photogenerated charge. To further examine the bulk charge transport properties, the charge lifetime (τn) can be determined by the relationship between charge density (Q) and decay time (t), according to a simple exponential function as follows [17,18]:
(6)
Q
( t )
=
A 0
+
A n
e
- t /
τ n

As a result, the charge lifetime (τn) raised from 96 ms of CuSCN to 138 ms of CuS QDs/CuSCN. Apparently, the prolonged lifetime profited from reduced charge recombination by constructing heterojunction.

Subsequently, IMPS measurement was employed to analyze the charge transfer kinetics across the semiconductor/electrolyte interface [50]. The rate constants corresponding to the charge transfer (ktrans ) and recombination (krec ) were derived from the normalized IMPS spectra, as presented at Fig. 5 b. The semicircles of both samples were located in the negative imaginary quadrant, as expected for p-type semiconductor. As discussed elsewhere, the high frequency response and low frequency region corresponded to the charge bulk transport and the competition between interfacial charge transfer and recombination, respectively [51]. Consequently, the charge lifetime (
τ
d ), rate constants and charge transfer efficiency (
η
trans ) at the interface were estimated using the equations below [52–54]:
(7)
τ d
=
1 /
2 π
f min
(8)
ω max
=
k
trans
+
k
rec
= 2 π
f max
(9)
η
trans
=
k
trans
k
trans
+
k
rec
where fmin represented the frequency at the minimal value in the IMPS, fmax was the frequency at the maximum of IMPS response in the upper quadrant and low frequency range. According to formula 7,
τ
d was estimated to be about 0.37 and 0.47 ms for pure CuSCN and CuS QDs/CuSCN respectively. The distinctly prolonged lifetime indicated less bulk recombination. As discussed above, the ktrans could be supposed to be similar when the externally applied bias was negative enough. Accordingly, the exclusive determining factor of krec was attributed to the fmax , as displayed in equation
(8) . Thus, the surface charge recombination rate constant was efficiently cut down after introducing CuS QDs. As a result, the
η
trans was enhanced (equation
(9) ). In a brief summary, the auxiliary CuS QDs could simultaneously prolong charge lifetime and restrict charge recombination. We attributed this phenomenon to the reduced defects caused by the passivation effects.

Finally, Mott-Schottky plots were carried out in Fig. 6
a. The apparent negative slopes verified that both samples demonstrated as p-type semiconductors, which was consistent with the aforementioned result. Notably, a considerably smaller slope was observed for CuS QDs/CuSCN composition than that of pure CuSCN, suggesting an increased charge carrier density that could be deduced from Mott-Schottky plots by fitting the equation:
(10)
N d
=
2 /
e 0
ε
ε 0
d ( 1 /
C 2
) / d V
- 1
where e0 ,
ε
,
ε
0 and Nd were defined as electron charge, dielectric constant, permittivity of vacuum and carrier density, respectively. Besides, d(1/C 2)/dV represented the slope from Mott-Schottky plot. It’s worth noting that the improved carrier density was beneficial to charge transport. Likewise, we employed EIS analyses to investigate the charge transport and transfer, as illustrated in Fig. 6 b. Compared to pure CuSCN, the smaller semicircular arc radius of CuS QDs/CuSCN also confirmed better charge transport.

In summary, the obstacles originated from poor charge transport and abundant defects for CuSCN have been solved successfully by introducing the in-situ grown CuS QDs with a simple one-step electrodeposition technique. On the basis of our results and analysis, a tentative mechanism of the formed Z-Scheme heterojunction was proposed to be responsible for substantially accelerated charge transport and restrained charge recombination. Besides, the decorated CuS QDs were also verified as an effective strategy to passivate defects by IMPS, Mott-Schottky and optical-electrical measurements. As a result, the bulk charge lifetime and surface transfer efficiency were promoted by almost 1.27 and 3.69 times in comparison to pure CuSCN. More importantly, the significance of this study lies in the fact that the facile one-step electrodeposition approach was anticipated to adapt in industry for large-scale production.

Pei Ning: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Junhui Liang: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Linghui Li: Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Da Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology. Laishun Qin: Formal analysis. Xin Yao: Formal analysis. Huayu Chen: Formal analysis. Yuexiang Huang: Formal analysis, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51872271, 51972294, 51972291) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20F040007, LQ19F040004, LY19E020003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.12.126.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrodeposited CuSCN metal-semiconductor-metal high performance deep-ultraviolet photodetector

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.P. Lin, X.J. Lin, D.C. Perng, Electrodeposited CuSCN metal-semiconductor-metal high performance deep-ultraviolet photodetector, Appl. Phys. Lett. 112 (2018) 021107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perovskite solar cells with CuSCN hole extraction layers yield stabilized efficiencies greater than 20%

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Arora, M.I. Dar, A. Hinderhofer, N. Pellet, F. Schreiber, S.M. Zakeeruddin, M. Grätzel, Perovskite solar cells with CuSCN hole extraction layers yield stabilized efficiencies greater than 20%, Science 358 (2017) 768-771.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview on enhancing the stability of lead halide perovskite quantum dots and their applications in phosphor-converted LEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wei, Z. Cheng, J. Lin, An overview on enhancing the stability of lead halide perovskite quantum dots and their applications in phosphor-converted LEDs, Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 310-350.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct solar-to-hydrogen conversion via inverted metamorphic multi-junction semiconductor architectures
